title: examples: add graceful shutdown example
body:
## Summary

- Adds a `graceful-shutdown` example demonstrating `tokio::signal::ctrl_c()` for clean server shutdown
- TCP echo server that notifies active connections via `broadcast` channel before exiting
- Shows the `tokio::select!` pattern for combining accept loops with shutdown signals

### Motivation

Graceful shutdown is one of the most common patterns in production async applications, but none of the existing 20 examples cover `tokio::signal` or shutdown coordination. This fills that gap with a minimal, self-contained example.

### Checklist

- [x] `cargo build --example graceful-shutdown`
- [x] `cargo run --example graceful-shutdown` — tested with `nc` + Ctrl+C
- [x] `cargo clippy --example graceful-shutdown`
- [x] `cargo fmt --check`
author: Sim-hu

commits.message:
examples: rework graceful shutdown to use CancellationToken + TaskTracker

Rewrite the example to follow the patterns described in the Graceful
Shutdown tutorial page. Uses CancellationToken to signal tasks and
TaskTracker to wait for in-flight connections instead of a sleep.

Also handles accept() errors without crashing and properly checks
write_all results.

fix: document accept error trade-off in graceful shutdown example

Add a comment explaining why `continue` is used for accept errors
and noting that a production server should consider breaking or
backing off on fatal errors to avoid busy loops.
refactor: extract handle_connection and make write_all cancellable

refactor: split handle_connection into outer cancel + inner echo fn

Flatten the nested select! blocks by extracting echo logic into a
separate async fn. The outer function handles cancellation via the
token while the inner function does plain read/write.

refactor: add timeout to shutdown notification write

A slow or unresponsive client could block the write_all during
shutdown indefinitely. Wrap it in a 1-second timeout and add a
comment explaining the rationale.
style: apply rustfmt to graceful-shutdown example

refactor: extract notify_shutdown into its own function

Move the shutdown notification logic into a dedicated async fn with a
doc-comment explaining when it is called. Also add a doc-comment to
echo for clarity.

title: net: use null get_peer_cred on Hurd
body:
## Motivation

Make tokio more usable on GNU/Hurd. Currently, users of tokio may fail to build because `get_peer_cred()` is not implemented.

## Solution

Due to the lack of (non-portable) APIs for this task (which makes it not possible to use any of the existing implementations), for now use the the null/empty `get_peer_cred()` implementation.
author: pinotree

commits.message:
net: use null get_peer_cred on Hurd

None of the other implementations/APIs is supported on the Hurd,
so for now use the null/empty get_peer_cred implementation.

title: ci: workaround for OpenOptionsExt in 1.94.0
body:
## Motivation

There was an accidental breaking change in `std::os::windows::OpenOptionsExt` in rust 1.94 (see #7955). The fix seems like it might take awhile to land and be released so I made this PR to allow tokio's CI to keep working on stable in the meantime in case it's useful to the project.

## Solution

Instead of using extension traits for mock_open_options, implement the extension methods on OpenOptions itself. This should be robust against any future issues too (I did it for both Windows and Unix options).
author: ChrisDenton

commits.message:
fs: only use inherent methods for mock OpenOptions

Instead of using extension traits for mock_open_options, implement the extension methods on OpenOptions itself.
ci: freeze rust on 1.93.1 for wasip1-threads
Add comment with link to issue

title: docs(runtime): document FD table pre-warming workaround
body:
Related:#2702, #7970

## Motivation

On Linux, the kernel grows the per-process file descriptor table lazily. The growth operation uses RCU synchronization, which in a multi-threaded process can block the calling thread for tens of milliseconds while it waits for all RCU readers to quiesce. When this happens on a tokio worker thread (e.g. during a `socket()` syscall), the entire event loop stalls, not just the affected task.

This was first identified by @carllerche in #2702 (p100 dropped from ~29ms to ~291µs after pre-warming). Multiple teams have independently discovered the same `dup`/`close` workaround, but it isn't documented anywhere in tokio.

## Solution

Adds an example (`examples/prewarm-fd-table.rs`) demonstrating the workaround: use `fcntl(fd, F_DUPFD_CLOEXEC, target)` to force the kernel to expand the FD table before building the runtime. The kernel never shrinks the table, so the capacity persists after closing the duplicated FDs.

Also starts a `Performance tuning` section in the `runtime` docs, with a small note on fd prewarming.
author: jlizen

reviews.body:
Looks good to me.

Shall we use this commit message？
```
examples: add FD table pre-warming example
```

commits.message: feat(examples): add FD table pre-warming example
docs(runtime): add Performance tuning section with FD table pre-warming note

Apply suggestions from code review

Co-authored-by: Martin Grigorov <martin-g@users.noreply.github.com>
fix(examples): use i32 for FD target, mention RLIMIT_NOFILE, fix non-Linux build
fix(examples): remove unused non-Linux stub for prewarm_fd_table
docs(examples): add safe stdlib alternative for FD table pre-warming

title: benches: use `is_multiple_of` instead of manual modulo check in `remote_spawn`
body:
Fixes a clippy::manual_is_multiple_of warning in the remote_spawn benchmark.

\`TOTAL_TASKS % 64 == 0\` can be written as \`TOTAL_TASKS.is_multiple_of(64)\` which is more readable.

commits.message:
bench: use is_multiple_of instead of manual modulo check

Addresses clippy::manual_is_multiple_of warning on latest stable.

title: ci: patch workspace members to disambiguate `--package`
body:
<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

Before the change, testing workspace members fails with `specification is ambiguous`

```console
$ cargo test -p tokio-stream # cargo nextest run -p tokio-stream
error: specificationm `tokio-stream` is ambiguous
help: re-run this command with one of the following specifications
  path+file:///home/figsoda/tokio/tokio-stream#0.1.18
  registry+https://github.com/rust-lang/crates.io-index#tokio-stream@0.1.18
```

This can be fixed with `cargo test -p file://$PWD/tokio-stream`, but it seems unnecessarily complicated

## Solution

Patch the other workspace members, just like what we are doing with `tokio`
author: figsoda

reviews.body: With the CI changes, it makes sense to me, though this still requires a formal approval from a maintainer

commits.message: chore: patch workspace members to disambiguate `--package`

title: runtime: add `worker_index()` getter
body:
<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

I am working on some runtime tracing instrumentation and one of the current pain points is being able to figure out what worker is currently running so that it can be properly correlated with the runtime metrics. I am working around this currently by enumerating `RuntimeMetrics::worker_thread_id` but that's obviously not ideal.
<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

## Solution

Add `tokio::runtime::worker_id()` which returns the 0-based index of the current runtime worker thread. The index matches the worker indices used by `RuntimeMetrics` (e.g. `worker_total_busy_duration`), making it possible to correlate per-worker metrics with specific tasks.

Returns `None` from blocking threads, the `block_on` caller thread (on the multi-thread runtime), and non-Tokio threads. For the current-thread runtime, always returns `Some(0)`.

<!--
Summarize the solution and provide any necessary context needed to understand
the code change.
-->
author: rcoh

reviews.body: Shall we use `worker_index()` instead of `worker_id()`? It looks like methods like `worker_local_schedule_count` are using the term "worker index".

Overall looks good to me, just one question. 

By the way, please sync the changes from the base branch as we have recently fixed a bug which may causes CI failure.
Looks good to me. Synced with the base branch via the GitHub web UI—let’s see if CI is green.

commits.message:
runtime: add `worker_id()` getter

Add `tokio::runtime::worker_id()` which returns the 0-based index of
the current runtime worker thread. The index matches the worker indices
used by `RuntimeMetrics` (e.g. `worker_total_busy_duration`), making
it possible to correlate per-worker metrics with specific tasks.

Returns `None` from blocking threads, the `block_on` caller thread
(on the multi-thread runtime), and non-Tokio threads. For the
current-thread runtime, always returns `Some(0)`.

title: bench: add remote_spawn benchmark for inject queue contention
body:
Add a benchmark that measures contention on the scheduler's inject queue mutex (push_remote_task) when multiple external threads spawn tasks into the runtime simultaneously. Every rt.spawn() from a non-worker thread unconditionally goes through push_remote_task, so this directly measures the scalability of the inject queue.

Results on an M1 Max MacBook Pro (10 cores), spawning 12,800 total tasks:

  threads/1:  3.39 ms  (265 ns/task, 1.00x)
  threads/2:  4.74 ms  (370 ns/task, 1.40x)
  threads/4:  5.89 ms  (460 ns/task, 1.74x)
  threads/8:  8.10 ms  (633 ns/task, 2.39x)

Wall-clock time increases with more threads despite constant total work, confirming the single mutex serializes producers.

<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

## Solution

<!--
Summarize the solution and provide any necessary context needed to understand
the code change.
-->
author: alex

commits.message:
bench: add remote_spawn benchmark for inject queue contention

Add a benchmark that measures contention on the scheduler's inject queue
mutex (push_remote_task) when multiple external threads spawn tasks into
the runtime simultaneously. Every rt.spawn() from a non-worker thread
unconditionally goes through push_remote_task, so this directly measures
the scalability of the inject queue.

Results on an M1 Max MacBook Pro (10 cores), spawning 12,800 total tasks:

  threads/1:  3.39 ms  (265 ns/task, 1.00x)
  threads/2:  4.74 ms  (370 ns/task, 1.40x)
  threads/4:  5.89 ms  (460 ns/task, 1.74x)
  threads/8:  8.10 ms  (633 ns/task, 2.39x)

Wall-clock time increases with more threads despite constant total work,
confirming the single mutex serializes producers.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

bench: use iter_custom to measure only spawn time, drop enable_all

Use iter_custom to time only the spawn phase (push_remote_task
contention), excluding the await/cleanup of join handles. Also remove
enable_all() since the benchmark doesn't need IO or timers.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: loom: remove `StaticAtomicU64`
body:
This PR addresses the TODO in tokio/src/loom/mocked.rs:65: <https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/9dacb1c53e341b1183a89800d46ee33c6eabf7ad/tokio/src/loom/mocked.rs#L65-L66>

## Motivation

When running under `cfg(loom)`, `StaticAtomicU64` previously fell back to `std::sync::atomic::AtomicU64`. Because this bypasses loom's concurrency model, static atomic operations were not fully exercised by loom, which reduced coverage for code paths such as thread and task ID allocation.

## Solution

This PR replaces the std fallback with a loom-compatible implementation of `StaticAtomicU64`, allowing loom to explore all relevant interleavings while preserving the existing API and usage patterns.

The reason this wasn't replaced with [loom::sync::atomic::AtomicU64](https://docs.rs/loom/latest/loom/sync/atomic/struct.AtomicU64.html) is because it doesn't have const initialization.

Implementation notes:
- Accept `Ordering` arguments to match the API, though actual ordering semantics are not modeled, but operations are serialized via a Mutex.
- `compare_exchange_weak` behaves like `compare_exchange` (no spurious failures), which should be sufficient for loom testing.
- Back the static storage with `OnceLock<Mutex<u64>>` (under `cfg(loom)`) to support safe const initialization and deterministic simulation.
- Implement the full set of required atomic operations: `load`, `store`, `fetch_add`, `compare_exchange`, and `compare_exchange_weak`.
- Match the behavior and public API of the existing non-loom `StaticAtomicU64` variants to avoid changes at call sites.
- Note that while this implementation uses std::sync::OnceLock internally, this is compatible with loom testing as loom itself [wraps std::sync::Mutex](https://docs.rs/loom/latest/src/loom/sync/mutex.rs.html#8-11). The only limitation would be if StaticAtomicU64 needed to hold non-std types where loom doesn't provide equivalents.

Tests:
- Add loom tests in `sync::tests::loom_atomic_static_u64` covering basic atomic semantics, concurrent access patterns, const initialization, and compare-exchange behavior.
- All existing loom tests continue to pass.
author: walker84837

commits.message:
loom: implement loom-compatible StaticAtomicU64

Replace the std::sync::atomic::AtomicU64 fallback used during loom
testing with a loom-aware implementation so loom can exercise static
atomic interleavings. Adds unit tests covering basic atomic operations.

loom: add docs, Debug and Default impls

Document all atomic operations in loom's StaticAtomicU64 to clarify
that the Ordering parameter is ignored, since all operations are
implemented using a Mutex internally.

This commit also adds Debug and Default impls to improve usability and
testability.

sync: correct expected value in concurrent test

This commit fixes the final assertion in
static_atomic_u64_concurrent_access to accurately reflect the
contributions from both threads: thread1 adds 10, thread2 adds
5*2 = 10, resulting in a total of 20.

loom: fix markdown formatting in StaticAtomicU64 docs

Add backticks around `compare_*` functions in the documentation comment
to clarify that it refers to the function, not plain text.

This is a minor doc change to satisfy spellcheck and improve
readability.

runtime: fix ThreadId::next panic under loom tests

StaticAtomicU64::new is technically const, but under loom its runtime
tracking requires lazy initialization. Use lazy_static! for loom test
builds to avoid panics, while retaining normal static initialization
for non-loom builds.

runtime: remove leftover loom static init comment

The comment explaining the use of `lazy_static!` for `NEXT_ID` under
loom testing was a leftover from local experimentation and does not add
value to the actual code. Removing it cleans up the source from
unnecessary noise.

runtime: use loom's AtomicU64 in thread_id.rs

This commit changes NEXT_ID's type from `StaticAtomicU64` to `AtomicU64`
when both `test` and `loom` cfg flags are enabled.

Since `lazy_static!` is used under these flags, const-safe
initialization isn't required, making AtomicU64 simpler.

runtime: remove unused StaticAtomicU64 import in tests

StaticAtomicU64 is no longer used under cfg(all(test, loom)). This
commit moves its import to the non-loom path to satisfy `-D warnings`.

loom: clarify docs for StaticAtomicU64 compare_exchange_weak

Update the docstring to accurately state that compare_exchange_weak
does not fail spuriously. Because StaticAtomicU64 synchronizes via a
Mutex<u64> the compare-and-exchange operation is deterministic with
respect to success or failure.

loom: remove StaticAtomicU64, use std & loom AtomicU64 accordingly

Remove the StaticAtomicU64 abstraction layer. In production builds, use
std::sync::atomic::AtomicU64 directly (which supports const
initialization). In loom tests, use loom::sync::atomic::AtomicU64 with
lazy_static! since loom's AtomicU64 does not support const
initialization.

This simplifies the codebase by removing an unnecessary abstraction
layer that was only needed because loom's AtomicU64 lacked const
support.

runtime: use crate::loom::sync::atomic::Ordering

Use the loom abstraction layer for Ordering to be consistent with the
rest of the codebase. This ensures the code works correctly in both
production and loom test builds.

runtime: use crate::loom for AtomicU64 outside of cfg(loom, test)

The crate::loom abstraction layer handles both 64-bit platforms (direct
AtomicU64) and 32-bit platforms (Mutex fallback). This fixes the build
on 32-bit targets like i686-unknown-linux-gnu.

runtime: consolidate loom atomic imports in id.rs

Combine multiple imports from crate::loom::sync::atomic into a single
import statement for simplicity.

runtime: consolidate loom atomic imports in thread_id.rs

Combine multiple imports from crate::loom::sync::atomic into a single
import statement for simplicity.

title: chore: prepare Tokio v1.50.0
body:
# 1.50.0 (Mar 3rd, 2026)

### Added

- net: add `TcpStream::set_zero_linger` ([#7837])
- rt: add `is_rt_shutdown_err` ([#7771])

### Changed

- io: add optimizer hint that `memchr` returns in-bounds pointer ([#7792])
- io: implement vectored writes for `write_buf` ([#7871])
- runtime: panic when `event_interval` is set to 0 ([#7838])
- runtime: shorten default thread name to fit in Linux limit ([#7880])
- signal: remember the result of `SetConsoleCtrlHandler` ([#7833])
- signal: specialize windows `Registry` ([#7885])

### Fixed

- io: always cleanup `AsyncFd` registration list on deregister ([#7773])
- macros: remove (most) local `use` declarations in `tokio::select!` ([#7929])
- net: fix `GET_BUF_SIZE` constant for `target_os = "android"` ([#7889])
- runtime: avoid redundant unpark in current_thread scheduler ([#7834])
- runtime: don't park in `current_thread` if `before_park` defers waker ([#7835])
- io: fix write readiness on ESP32 on short writes ([#7872])
- runtime: wake deferred tasks before entering `block_in_place` ([#7879])
- sync: drop rx waker when oneshot receiver is dropped ([#7886])
- runtime: fix double increment of `num_idle_threads` on shutdown ([#7910], [#7918], [#7922])

### Unstable

- fs: check for io-uring opcode support ([#7815])
- runtime: avoid lock acquisition after uring init ([#7850])

### Documented

- docs: update outdated unstable features section ([#7839])
- io: clarify the behavior of `AsyncWriteExt::shutdown()` ([#7908])
- io: explain how to flush stdout/stderr ([#7904])
- io: fix incorrect and confusing `AsyncWrite` documentation ([#7875])
- rt: clarify the documentation of `Runtime::spawn` ([#7803])
- rt: fix missing quotation in docs ([#7925])
- runtime: correct the default thread name in docs ([#7896])
- runtime: fix `event_interval` doc ([#7932])
- sync: clarify RwLock fairness documentation ([#7919])
- sync: clarify that `recv` returns `None` once closed and no more messages ([#7920])
- task: clarify when to use `spawn_blocking` vs dedicated threads ([#7923])
- task: doc that task drops before `JoinHandle` completion ([#7825])
- signal: guarantee that listeners never return `None` ([#7869])
- task: fix task module feature flags in docs ([#7891])
- task: fix two typos ([#7913])
- task: improve the docs of `Builder::spawn_local` ([#7828])
- time: add docs about auto-advance and when to use sleep ([#7858])
- util: fix typo in docs ([#7926])

title: util: add `write_all_vectored` to `tokio-util`
body:
## Motivation

Closes #3679 

Was recently looking for `write_all_vectored` and found it missing, went to add it before realizing the MSRV is not there yet.

I thought I'd submit this anyway, when the MSRV version moves ahead you can accept, or we could copy in the `IoSlice::advance_slices` fn until that happens?

## Solution

~~Tried to follow existing pattern for `write_x` methods~~

title: sync: fix mpsc Disconnection docs to clarify recv returns None only when channel is empty
body:
Fixes #6053

## Problem

The `# Disconnection` section of the mpsc module docs stated:

> As such, `Receiver::poll` returns `Ok(Ready(None))`.

This is incorrect in two ways:

1. **Wrong condition**: When all `Sender` handles are dropped, `recv` does NOT immediately return `None`. Messages already buffered in the channel can still be received. `recv` only returns `None` once all senders are dropped **and** the channel has been fully drained.

2. **Wrong method name**: `Receiver::poll` is not a public API method. The public methods are `Receiver::recv` and `Receiver::poll_recv`.

## Fix

Update the documentation to accurately state that `recv` returns `None` (and `poll_recv` returns `Poll::Ready(None)`) only once all senders have been dropped **and** all buffered values have been received.
author: cluster2600

commits.message:
sync: fix mpsc Disconnection docs to clarify recv returns None only when channel is empty

The previous documentation stated:
  'As such, Receiver::poll returns Ok(Ready(None))'

This was misleading: when all Sender handles are dropped, recv does NOT
immediately return None. Buffered messages already in the channel can
still be received. Only after all senders are dropped AND the channel
has been fully drained does recv return None.

Also update the method reference from the internal Receiver::poll to the
public API: Receiver::recv and Receiver::poll_recv.

Closes #6053

title: Fix `event_interval` doc
body:
## Motivation

The less frequently tasks yield and transfer control back to the runtime, the less chances the runtime has for dealing with I/O events, meaning users should probably lower this value if this were the case.
The current doc seemed to have a typo here.

## Solution

Fix wording.

title: runtime: fix race condition during the blocking pool shutdown
body:
Follow-up of <https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/pull/7918#issuecomment-3928245159>.

Consider this sequence:

1. A blocking thread is sleeping at [`pool.rs:525`].
2. Someone enqueues a task, then decreases the `num_idle_thread`, finally calls `condvar.notify_one` at [`pool.rs:449-451`].
3. Another one shutdown the blocking pool, flips the `shared.shutdown` bit, finally unlock the `shared` at [`pool.rs:254-261`].
4. That blocking thread wakes at [`pool.rs:525`], and exit the loop at [`pool.rs:535`]. At this time, the `num_idle_thread` has already been decreased at [`pool.rs:449`].
5. That blocking thread tries to decrease the `num_idle_thread` again at [`pool.rs:574`], then panic.


[`pool.rs:525`]:https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L525
[`pool.rs:449-451`]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L449-L451
[`pool.rs:254-261`]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L254-L261
[`pool.rs:535`]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L535
[`pool.rs:449`]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L449
[`pool.rs:574`]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/e65040f061008ca5a94d21721aa8b544c078c606/tokio/src/runtime/blocking/pool.rs#L574
author: ADD-SP

reviews.body:
LGTM!

Could this be tested reliably with loom ?

commits.message: runtime: fix race condition during the blocking pool shutdown
add a test for this race condition

title: stream: work around the rustc bug in `StreamExt::collect`
body:
This is a fix for #7753.

As stated in that issue, there is a rustc bug, which can cause issues when interacting with types that have a manual `unsafe impl Send` and which contains a parameter set to `dyn Any`.

This PR solves the issue by removing the trait bounds on the struct itself, which makes `rustc` able to correctly infer that the combined struct is `Send`.

title: task: clarify when to use spawn_blocking vs dedicated threads
body:
## Motivation

The current spawn_blocking documentation states that the function is intended for operations that eventually finish and that thread::spawn should be used for ordinary threads.
However, the practical distinction between bounded blocking work and long-lived or persistent workers is not explicit. This often leads to confusion about when long-running tasks should use spawn_blocking versus a dedicated thread.
This clarification was requested following discussion #7878 .

## Solution

Add a short guidance section to the spawn_blocking documentation explaining:
  - spawn_blocking is intended for short-lived, bounded blocking work
  - Long-lived or persistent blocking tasks reduce the effective capacity of the blocking thread pool
  - For such workloads, a dedicated thread via std::thread::spawn is preferred
The change is documentation-only and does not modify behavior.
author: 0rlych1kk4

commits.message: docs(task): clarify when to use spawn_blocking vs dedicated threads
docs(task): add guidance when to use spawn_blocking vs dedicated threads
docs(task): reorganize spawn_blocking docs per review

title: runtime: fix TOCTOU issue when decreasing `num_idle_threads`
body: Follow-up of . FIx a flaky test: --- This bug was introduced in , which changed the metrics from a ordinary counter to atomic counter, so there is a [TOCTOU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time-of-check_to_time-of-use) issue. Apparently, we can catch the underflow/overflow issue via CAS loop. However, it will be a significant change, thus I prefer to delete this assertion to address the flaky test at first. But I would highly suggest to check the underflow/overflow in the implementation of `MetricAtomicUsize` later, and also measure the performance impact. Correctness should over the performance. Or introducing something like `checked_sub` for caller to optimize the performance.
author: ADD-SP

commits.message: runtime: fix TOCTOU issue when decreasing `num_idle_threads`
add the assertion back and fix it

title: sync: clarify RwLock fairness documentation
body:
Fixes #6901

## Problem

The previous wording in the `RwLock` documentation was misleading:

> if a task that wishes to acquire the write lock is **at the head of the queue**, read locks will not be given out until the write lock has been released

This implies that readers are only blocked when the writer is at position #1 in the queue. However, due to the FIFO ordering policy, any write request queued *before* a read request will block that reader — the writer's position (head or not) is irrelevant from the reader's perspective.

**Example:** If R1 → W → R2 are queued in that order, R2 is blocked by W even though W is not at the head of the queue. Once R1 gets the lock (it was already queued before W), R2 cannot skip past W.

## Fix

Replace with a more accurate description:

> a read lock will not be given out until all write lock requests that were queued before it have been acquired and released

This clearly communicates the FIFO semantics without the misleading "head of the queue" implication.

This rephrasing was suggested in the issue by @cip999 and acknowledged as reasonable by @Darksonn.
author: cluster2600

reviews.body: This seems ok to me. Thanks!

commits.message:
sync: clarify RwLock fairness documentation

The previous wording 'if a task that wishes to acquire the write lock is
at the head of the queue, read locks will not be given out' was
misleading: it implied that readers are only blocked when the writer is
first in the queue. In reality, due to the FIFO ordering, any write
request queued *before* a read request will block that reader.

Replace with a more accurate description: 'a read lock will not be given
out until all write lock requests that were queued before it have been
acquired and released.'

Closes #6901

title: replace some futures with `poll_fn`
body: These were found by going through all matches from the " Future for " search query.
author: vilgotf

title: runtime: fix double-increment of num_idle_threads on blocking pool shutdown
body:
When a blocking pool worker thread hits the shutdown path, `num_idle_threads` gets incremented twice but only decremented once on thread exit, leaving the metric over-counted.

**What happens:**

The worker loop increments `num_idle_threads` when entering IDLE state (before the condvar wait). During shutdown, `condvar.notify_all()` wakes all idle threads, but unlike a normal task spawn, it does *not* decrement `num_idle_threads` or increment `num_notify`. The woken thread sees `shared.shutdown == true`, exits the inner while loop, and enters the `if shared.shutdown` block which had a second `inc_num_idle_threads()` call. On thread exit, only one `dec_num_idle_threads()` happens, so the net effect is +1 per idle thread at shutdown time.

**The fix:**

Remove the extra `inc_num_idle_threads()` in the shutdown path. The thread is already counted as idle from the increment at the top of the IDLE section, so the balance is: +1 on entering IDLE, -1 on thread exit.

Closes #6439
author: veeceey

commits.message:
runtime: fix double-increment of num_idle_threads during blocking pool shutdown

When a blocking pool worker thread enters the shutdown path, it has
already incremented num_idle_threads upon entering the IDLE state at
the top of the main loop. The extra inc_num_idle_threads() call inside
the `if shared.shutdown` block incorrectly increments the counter a
second time, since the shutdown notification (condvar.notify_all) does
not decrement num_idle like a normal task spawn does.

This causes num_idle_threads to be over-counted during shutdown: each
idle worker thread that goes through the shutdown path adds an extra
+1 that is never balanced out. The subsequent dec_num_idle_threads()
on thread exit only removes one of the two increments.

Remove the spurious increment so the idle count stays consistent.

Closes #6439

test: add regression test for num_idle_blocking_threads after shutdown

Verify that the num_idle_blocking_threads metric returns 0 after runtime
shutdown. Before the fix in the previous commit, the shutdown path would
increment num_idle_threads a second time for each idle worker thread,
leaving the counter over-counted.

Refs: #6439

title: tests: skip issue_7144 if strace is missing
body:
## Motivation

The unit test `issue_7144` requires `strace` to run. On systems where it is not installed, the test fails, causing the test suite to fail unexpectedly, and currently triggers `unwrap()`: <https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/9dacb1c53e341b1183a89800d46ee33c6eabf7ad/tokio/tests/process_issue_7144.rs#L21-L24>

This is an environment-dependent issue, not a problem with the library or unit test itself, and makes local testing less reliable.

This change aims to make the test suite pass consistently, regardless of whether `strace` is present.

## Solution

The test now checks if `strace` is available before running. If `strace` is missing, the test is skipped gracefully with no failure.

A check was added and it uses only `tokio::process::Command`. This change makes local testing more reliable without affecting the behavior of the test on systems where `strace` is present.
author: walker84837

commits.message:
tests: skip issue_7144 if strace is missing

The unit test `issue_7144` requires `strace` and will fail if it is not
installed. This patch skips the test gracefully when `strace` is
missing, making the test suite pass on systems without it.

Refs: #7144

title: add wasm32-wasip2 networking support
body:
## Motivation

This adds networking support for the `wasm32-wasip2` target platform, which includes more extensive support for sockets than `wasm32-wasip1`.

## Solution

The bulk of the changes are in https://github.com/tokio-rs/mio/pull/1931.  This patch mainly tweaks a few `cfg` directives to indicate `wasm32-wasip2`'s additional capabilities.

~~Note that this is a draft PR until https://github.com/tokio-rs/mio/pull/1931 and https://github.com/rust-lang/socket2/pull/639 have been include in stable releases of their respective projects.~~

Also note that I've added a `wasm32-wasip2` target to CI and triaged each test which was previously disabled for WASI into one of three categories:

- Disabled on both WASIp1 and p2 due to not-yet-supported features such as multithreading
- Disabled on p1 but enabled on p2
- Disabled on p1 and _temporarily_ disabled on p2 due to `wasi-libc` bugfixes which have been merged but not yet included in a Rust release.  I'll open an issue to re-enable them when the fixes land in Rust.

## Future Work

In the future, we could consider adding support for `tokio::net::lookup_host`. WASIp2 natively supports asynchronous DNS lookups and is single threaded, whereas Tokio currently assumes DNS lookups are blocking and require multithreading to emulate async lookups.  A WASIp2-specific implementation could do the lookup directly without multithreading.

WASIp2 also supports single-threaded, asynchronous file I/O, timers, etc.  We could either support those directly or wait for WASIp3's multithreading support, in which case most of `tokio::fs` (as well as `tokio::net::lookup_host`, etc.) _should_ work unchanged via `wasi-libc` and worker threads.

Currently, building for WASIp2 requires `RUSTFLAGS="--cfg tokio_unstable"`.  Once we have a solid maintenance plan, we can remove that requirement.
author: dicej

commits.message:
add wasm32-wasip2 networking support

Motivation

This adds networking support for the `wasm32-wasip2` target platform, which
includes more extensive support for sockets than `wasm32-wasip1`.

Solution

The bulk of the changes are in https://github.com/tokio-rs/mio/pull/1931.  This
patch mainly tweaks a few `cfg` directives to indicate `wasm32-wasip2`'s
additional capabilities.

Note that this is a draft PR until until
https://github.com/tokio-rs/mio/pull/1931 and
https://github.com/rust-lang/socket2/pull/639 have been include in stable
releases of their respective projects.

Also note that I've added a `wasm32-wasip2` target to CI and triaged each test
which was previously disabled for WASI into one of three categories:

- Disabled on both WASIp1 and p2 due to not-yet-supported features such as multithreading
- Disabled on p1 but enabled on p2
- Disabled on p1 and _temporarily_ disabled on p2 due to `wasi-libc` bugfixes which have been merged but not yet included in a Rust release.  I'll open an issue to re-enable them when the fixes land in Rust.

Future Work

In the future, we could consider adding support for `tokio::net::lookup_host`.
WASIp2 natively supports asynchronous DNS lookups and is single threaded,
whereas Tokio currently assumes DNS lookups are blocking and require
multithreading to emulate async lookups.  A WASIp2-specific implementation could
do the lookup directly without multithreading.

WASIp2 also supports single-threaded, asynchronous file I/O, timers, etc.  We
could either support those directly or wait for WASIp3's multithreading support,
in which case most of `tokio::fs` (as well as `tokio::net::lookup_host`, etc.)
_should_ work unchanged via `wasi-libc` and worker threads.

Currently, building for WASIp2 requires RUSTFLAGS="--cfg tokio_unstable"`.  Once
we have a solid maintenance plan, we can remove that requirement.

title: macros: improve overall macro hygiene
body:
## Motivation

Multiple macro expansions were previously assuming the existence of some
standard library symbols to both be in-scope and referring to the right symbol,
and not only having the same identifier.

## Solution

This has now been refactored into using reexports from the `support` module
under the `macros` module. Some other macro invocations were also using absolute
standard library paths instead of calling into the afore mentioned module
through the special `$crate` metavariable. This has been changed, thus also
reexporting some other items in `support`.
author: dybucc

reviews.body:
LGTM!

The fixed Pin/Ok/Err may break some users (e.g. hyper - https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/pull/7929#issuecomment-3943342335) but IMO it is OK.
We have to keep imports to avoid breaking users, but changing the actual uses to full paths sounds good to me.

commits.message:
macros: improve overall macro hygiene

Multiple macro expansions were previously assumming the existence of
some standard library symbols to both be in-scope and referring to the
right symbol, and not only having the same identifier.

This has now been refactored into using reexports from the `support`
module under the `macros` module. Some other macro invocations were also
using absolute standard library paths instead of calling into the afore
mentioned module through the special `$crate` macro. This has been
changed, thus also reexporting some other items in `support`.

title: sync: fix `notify_waiters` priority in `Notify`
body:
Fixes: #7965

<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

Previously, if a `notify_waiters()` was followed by a `notify_one()`, an unpolled `Notified` future created before the `notify_waiters()` call would consume the `NOTIFIED` permit created by the `notify_one()` call.





<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

## Solution


This commit fixes this by verifying the `notify_waiters_calls` count before optimistically attempting to acquire the `NOTIFIED` permit. If the count indicates a `notify_waiters()` call has already happened, the future transitions directly to `State::Done` and leaves the permit intact for other waiters.


<!--
Summarize the solution and provide any necessary context needed to understand
the code change.
-->
author: Natural-selection1

commits.message:
sync: fix `notify_waiters` priority in `Notify`

Previously, if a `notify_waiters()` was followed by a `notify_one()`,
an unpolled `Notified` future created before the `notify_waiters()`
call would consume the `NOTIFIED` permit created by the
`notify_one()` call.

This commit fixes this by verifying the `notify_waiters_calls` count
before optimistically attempting to acquire the `NOTIFIED` permit. If
the count indicates a `notify_waiters()` call has already happened, the
future transitions directly to `State::Done` and leaves the permit
intact for other waiters.

Fixes: #7965

body:
<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

After `LocalRuntime` was implemented as per the issue #6739 I think its time to stabilize it so we don't need to set the `RUSTFLAGS` when using it 

<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

## Solution

Remove cfg unstable in docs and places where it was initially implemented, I have tested the macro

```rust
#[tokio::main(flavor = "local")]
async fn main() {
    println!("hello");
}
```

And using it just like

```rust
use tokio::runtime::LocalRuntime;

fn main() {
    let rt = LocalRuntime::new().unwrap();

    rt.block_on(async {
        println!("{:?}", "hello");
    });

    println!("hello");
}
```

<!--
Summarize the solution and provide any necessary context needed to understand
the code change.
-->
author: Daksh14

commits.message: Fix macro and remove unstable from LocalRuntime locations

title: sync: fix panic in `Chan::recv_many` when called with non-empty vector on closed channel
body:
## Motivation

`Chan::recv_many` intends to assert that no slots
have been consumed when exiting with `Ready` via
the `rx_closed` code path.

Instead of asserting no items were added to the
buffer, it asserted buffer emptiness, incorrectly
making assumptions about the provided buffer.

When `recv_many` was called on an empty channel
with idle semaphore after the receiver was closed,
the method would panic.

## Solution

The branch coverage had been previously missing.

This changeset corrects the assertion
and adds tests covering the code path.

Fixes #7990.
author: LeoniePhiline

reviews.body:
Thanks!

> Merge without squash is recommended.

If you add the test first, then I will need to merge with squash. Otherwise bisection will not work on the main branch if someone hits the first commit in a bisection.

commits.message:
sync: fix panic in `Chan::recv_many` when called with non-empty vector on closed channel (#7991)

`Chan::recv_many` intends to assert that no slots
have been consumed when exiting with `Ready` via
the `rx_closed` code path.

Instead of asserting no items were added to the
buffer, it asserted buffer emptiness, incorrectly
making assumptions about the provided buffer.

When `recv_many` was called on an empty channel
with idle semaphore after the receiver was closed,
the method would panic.

The branch coverage had been previously missing.

This changeset corrects the assertion
and adds tests covering the code path.

Fixes #7990.

[#7991]: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/pull/7991
author: Darksonn

title: ci: test AArch64/Armv7hf Linux on ubuntu-22.04-arm runner
body:
<!--
Thank you for your Pull Request. Please provide a description above and review
the requirements below.

Bug fixes and new features should include tests.

Contributors guide: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md

The contributors guide includes instructions for running rustfmt and building the
documentation, which requires special commands beyond `cargo fmt` and `cargo doc`.
-->

## Motivation

<!--
Explain the context and why you're making that change. What is the problem
you're trying to solve? In some cases there is not a problem and this can be
thought of as being the motivation for your change.
-->

Currently, we are testing `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu` and `armv7-unknown-linux-gnueabihf` using QEMU, but QEMU is slow than native execution.

GitHub [now supports free AArch64 Linux runners as public preview](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-01-16-linux-arm64-hosted-runners-now-available-for-free-in-public-repositories-public-preview/), so this PR use it for them.

## Solution

As for `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`, it is enough to change the OS to run on, and as for `armv7-unknown-linux-gnueabihf`, it is necessary to change the OS to run on and install some additional packages, but the [latter is handled by the setup-cross-toolchain-action](https://github.com/taiki-e/setup-cross-toolchain-action/releases/tag/v1.27.0) currently already in use, so in practice it is also enough to change the OS to run on.

(As for `armv5te-unknown-linux-gnueabi`, Ubuntu does not provide packages for `armel`, so unlike `armv7-unknown-linux-gnueabihf` (`armhf`), we still need to use QEMU for now.)

This PR also removes CircleCI config (`.circleci/config.yml`) because native AArch64 test is now covered in GitHub Actions.

Note that this PR uses `ubuntu-22.04-arm` instead of `ubuntu-24.04-arm` due to upstream bug: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/135867
(I leaved TODO comments about it.)
author: taiki-e

commits.message: ci: test AArch64/Armv7hf Linux on ubuntu-22.04-arm runner
Remove .circleci/config.yml since native aarch64 test is now covered in github actions
